items.q: What is the difference between a silent and a missense mutation?
items.correct: A silent mutation changes a base but the codon still codes for the same amino acid; a missense mutation changes the amino acid encoded.
items.misconception: A silent mutation has no effect because no base actually changes.
items.random_wrong: Missense and silent mutations always produce a non-functional protein.
items.why: Students think 'silent' means 'no change at the DNA level' rather than 'no change at the protein level' (due to genetic code degeneracy).

items.q: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis?
items.correct: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes).
items.misconception: Mitosis and meiosis both produce gametes; they are basically the same.
items.random_wrong: Mitosis happens in plants and meiosis happens in animals.
items.why: The similar names lead to confusion; students mix up which process produces gametes vs body cells.

items.q: After pre-mRNA splicing in eukaryotes, which sequences remain in the mature mRNA?
items.misconception: Introns remain because they encode the protein; exons are removed as junk.
items.random_wrong: Both introns and exons are removed and the cell uses just the cap and tail.
items.why: Students confuse the EX- (expressed) and IN- prefixes; the naming is the opposite of what intuition suggests.

items.q: Which of these is a prokaryote?
items.why: It's Bacterium. “Human skin cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: aerobic respiration. “protein synthesis” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A bacterium is 2 µm long. How many fit end-to-end across a 1 mm gap?
items.why: Exact answer: 500 bacteria. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
